images.caption: Figure 1:Context unawareness manifests across both agentic and multimodal systems.Left:In an agentic code-editing setting, the model has access to the relevant source file but fails to maintain consistency with the surrounding context across edits. As a result, it removes the definition of variableiithat is subsequently referenced, causing a runtime error.Right:In a multimodal reasoning setting, the model fails to correctly ground its answer in the visual evidence. Although the relevant information is present in the figure, it misreads theyyvalue ofg​(x)g(x)as22rather than33asx→−1x\to-1, leading to an incorrect prediction.

Figure 2:Contrastive context selection given a query–answer pair.GPT-5.4 and Claude Opus 4.7 perform reliably, while Qwen3 (VL) and Qwen3.5 remain close to random choice despite strong performance on standard benchmarks.

Figure 3:Overview ofContextRL.We augment GRPO with a context-awareness loss built from contrastive context pairs. For each query-answer pair(Q,A)(Q,A), an anchor contextC​o​n​t​e​x​tContextAAsupports the answer, while a confounderC​o​n​t​e​x​tContextBBis superficially similar but inconsistent. Given the answer, the auxiliary objective trains the policy to select the supporting context over the confounder, improving context-aware reasoning in both coding and multimodal settings.

Figure 4:Contrastive context pairs construction pipeline.Left:Step-by-step filtering to mine contrastive trajectory pairs for theagentic setting.Right:Generative editing and similarity-based retrieval to mine contrastive image pairs for themultimodal setting. A concrete(C+,C−)(C^{+},C^{-})example is shown
alongside each method. Details can be found in Section2.1and2.2.

Table 1:Main results on 5 long-horizon benchmarks.ContextRLconsistently outperforms RL baseline across all the tasks for both base models. Notably,ContextRLdemonstrates robust generalization to long-context OOD tasks where standard outcome-based RL struggles or regresses.

Table 2:Main results on 12 diverse multimodal benchmarks.ContextRLachieves the best performance across all sub-categories, surpassing both the RL baseline and strong reference methods like PAPO. The broad improvements indicate thatContextRLsuccessfully translates enhanced visual grounding into stronger end-to-end reasoning capabilities.

Figure 5:Context-selection accuracy versus end-task performance.Thexx-axis denotes selection accuracy. Theyy-axis denotes end-task performance. Top-right is optimal. DA-SFT achieves high selection accuracy but collapses on theyy-axis. DA-RL fails to learn discrimination at all.ContextRLis theonlymethod that couples high context awareness with end-task improvements.
Figure 6:Subgroup composition of the multimodal training set (45,000 examples).Per-source counts within each subgroup are listed in Table6.
Figure 7:Effect of contrastive data proportion on Qwen2.5-VL-7B performance. The x-axis is data proportion and the y-axis is accuracy (%). The 15% setting achieves the best overall balance.
Figure 8:Effect of maximum response length on Qwen3-VL-8B performance. Max length 4096 offers the best average performance across all benchmarks.

Figure 9:Effect of the contrastive loss coefficientλ\lambdaon benchmark performance. Both models are stable across smallλ\lambdavalues but degrade on the majority of benchmarks onceλ\lambdais increased to 0.01.
